Dyslexia Services in Houston ISD

What are my child's dyslexia rights in Texas?
Under the Texas Dyslexia Handbook, all districts must screen students for dyslexia in grades K-2 and provide evidence-based intervention programs. If identified, your child is entitled to structured literacy instruction and—if eligible—a 504 Plan or IEP with accommodations.

The Texas Dyslexia Handbook (2024 Update)

Houston ISD must follow the Texas Dyslexia Handbook, which provides the legal framework for identifying and serving students with dyslexia.

The 2024 update (following HB 3928) clarifies that a dyslexia evaluation is now officially a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) under special education law. This gives parents stronger legal protections.

Key point: If your child is struggling to read, you do not have to "wait and see." You can request a formal dyslexia evaluation in writing at any time.

Mandatory Screening Requirements

Texas law requires Houston ISD to screen all students in kindergarten and first grade for dyslexia. The screening evaluates:

  • Phonological awareness
  • Rapid naming
  • Letter-sound knowledge

If your child shows risk during screening, the campus must notify you and begin monitoring. However, you don't need to wait for a screener to flag your child—you can request a full evaluation immediately.

Structured Literacy Programs

If your child is identified with dyslexia, Houston ISD must provide a "Structured Literacy" program—an explicit, systematic approach to reading instruction.

Structured Literacy programs emphasize:

  • Phonemic awareness
  • Phonics
  • Fluency
  • Vocabulary
  • Reading comprehension

Instruction must be sequential and cumulative, building on previously learned skills. The specific program used will be determined in collaboration with your ARD or 504 committee.

504 Plan vs. IEP for Dyslexia

A major point of confusion: Should your dyslexic child receive a 504 Plan or a full IEP?

The 2024 handbook clarifies that dyslexia is a Specific Learning Disability (SLD), which qualifies for special education services under IDEA.

Section 504 Plan:

  • Appropriate for students who need accommodations (extra time, audio support) but can access the general curriculum with minimal support
  • Provides accommodations but not specialized instruction
  • Fewer legal protections than an IEP

IEP (Special Education):

  • Required for students whose dyslexia significantly impacts learning and who need intensive, individualized, specialized instruction
  • Provides both accommodations AND specialized teaching
  • Stronger legal protections and goals

If your child's reading is significantly below grade level, push for an IEP, not just a 504.

How to Request a Dyslexia Evaluation

Do not rely on verbal conversations. To start the legal clock, submit a written request:

  1. Write the Request: Draft a letter requesting a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) for suspected dyslexia and Specific Learning Disabilities
  2. Submit to the Principal: Email directly to your campus principal and copy the Special Education Director
  3. Watch the Clock: Once received, the district has 15 school days to provide written response and consent form
  4. Prepare for Testing: After you sign consent, the district has 45 school days to complete the evaluation

What Happens If the School Refuses?

If Houston ISD refuses to evaluate, they must provide "Prior Written Notice" explaining why. You can challenge this refusal by:

  • Filing a formal grievance with the district
  • Requesting mediation through TEA
  • Filing for a due process hearing

The school cannot force your child to go through months of "RTI" before agreeing to evaluate for dyslexia.

Accommodations for Dyslexia

Whether your child has a 504 or IEP, common accommodations include:

  • Extended time on tests and assignments
  • Audio books and text-to-speech software
  • Preferential seating near instruction
  • Reduced copying from board
  • Use of graphic organizers
  • Tests read aloud

Discipline Rights & Grievances in Houston ISD

⚠️ Urgent: If your child has been suspended for more than 10 cumulative days in Houston ISD, this is legally a Change of Placement. The school must hold an ARD/MDR meeting immediately.
Is a 10-day suspension a Change of Placement?
Yes. Under IDEA, suspending a special education student for more than 10 cumulative school days in a year triggers a Change of Placement and requires a Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) within 10 business days.

The 10-Day Suspension Rule

If your child has an IEP or 504 plan, standard discipline rules do not fully apply. Houston ISD can suspend your child for up to 10 days total per academic year.

After the 10th day of suspension (even if they are separate 2-day or 3-day incidents), federal law triggers massive protections:

  • The district MUST provide educational services to maintain academic progress
  • A Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) is required before any further disciplinary action
  • The school cannot send your child home with nothing

Important: Informal removals count too. If the principal keeps calling you to "pick your child up early" for behavior, those count as suspension days.

What Is an MDR (Manifestation Determination Review)?

An MDR is a mandatory meeting that occurs before Houston ISD can expel your child or send them to DAEP (Disciplinary Alternative Education Program) for more than 10 days.

The ARD committee must answer two questions:

  1. Was the conduct caused by, or directly related to, the child's disability?
  2. Was the conduct the direct result of Houston ISD's failure to implement the IEP?

If the answer to EITHER question is yes, the school cannot discipline your child the same way they would a non-disabled student. They must:

  • Conduct a Functional Behavioral Assessment (FBA)
  • Return your child to their original placement
  • Add behavioral supports to the IEP

Filing a Level 1 Grievance in Houston ISD

If you believe a campus administrator violated your child's rights or ignored their IEP, file a formal grievance. Angry emails don't count.

Steps to file a grievance:

  1. Find the Policy: Houston ISD board policy (FNG Local) governs complaints. You typically have only 15 days from the incident to file.
  2. Get the Form: Request the official Level 1 Grievance form from the principal or download from the district website.
  3. Stick to Facts: State the facts, the specific policy or IEP provision violated, and the exact remedy you seek.
  4. Submit Officially: Submit to the campus principal via email (keep a time-stamped copy).

TEA State Complaints

If the grievance process fails and Houston ISD continues to deny your child a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE), escalate to the state.

You can file a formal State Complaint with the Texas Education Agency (TEA), which triggers a state investigator to review the district's actions.

When to file a TEA complaint:

  • District refuses to evaluate your child
  • IEP services are not being implemented
  • District violated procedural safeguards (no 5-day notice, refused 10-day recess, etc.)
  • Child is being illegally suspended

TEA must investigate and issue findings. If violations are found, Houston ISD must take corrective action.

Due Process Hearings

For severe placement disagreements or if TEA complaints don't resolve the issue, you can file for a Due Process Hearing—a formal legal trial before an administrative judge.

This is the most powerful legal tool parents have, but it requires significant preparation. Most parents hire a special education attorney for due process.

Common Violations to Watch For

  • IEP not implemented: Services listed on IEP aren't actually being provided
  • No progress on goals: Child makes no measurable progress year after year
  • Informal suspensions: Repeatedly sent home early without formal suspension paperwork
  • Refused evaluation: District refuses to test despite written parent request
  • No prior written notice: District makes changes without giving you formal notice

Protecting Your Child's Placement

While disputes are being resolved, your child has the right to "stay-put"—they remain in their current educational placement unless you agree otherwise or a hearing officer orders a change.

The school cannot unilaterally move your child to a more restrictive setting while you're fighting for services.

  4. A Support Person: You can bring an advocate, friend, or attorney

Understanding the IEP Document

The IEP is a legally binding contract. Pay attention to these sections:

  • Present Levels (PLAAFP): Your child's current performance. Push back if vague.
  • Annual Goals: Must be measurable. "Will improve reading" is not legal. "Will read 90 words per minute by May" is.
  • Services: Exact minutes per week for each service (Speech, Resource Room, etc.)
  • Accommodations: Extended time, preferential seating, graphic organizers, etc.

When You Disagree With the Team

You can disagree with the ARD committee's recommendations. If you disagree with initial placement, you can refuse consent—services cannot begin without your signature.

If you disagree with a revised IEP:

  • Do NOT sign "Agree"
  • Check "Disagree"
  • Request your 10-day recess
  • Ensure your concerns are written in the official minutes

You also have the right to request an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at district expense if you believe their testing is flawed.

After the ARD Meeting

Houston ISD must provide you with a copy of the finalized IEP within a reasonable time. Services should begin as soon as possible after you sign consent for initial services.

If the district fails to implement the IEP as written, document every violation and file a formal grievance or TEA complaint.

How to Request an Evaluation in Houston ISD

How long does Texas have to complete an evaluation?
Once Houston ISD receives your written evaluation request, they have exactly 45 school days to complete the Full Individual Evaluation (FIE) and hold an ARD meeting. Verbal requests don't start the clock—always submit in writing.

Requesting a Special Education Evaluation

If your child is struggling academically or you suspect a disability, you have the absolute legal right to request a special education evaluation from Houston ISD.

Under IDEA's "Child Find" provisions, Houston ISD must identify, locate, and evaluate all children with disabilities. Your formal written request triggers this legal obligation.

Important: Verbal requests to a teacher do not start the legal clock. You must put your evaluation request in writing.

How to Submit Your Written Request

Follow these steps to ensure your request is legally valid:

  1. Address It Correctly: Send the letter to both the campus principal and the Special Education Director
  2. Use the "Magic Words": State clearly: "I am requesting a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) under IDEA to determine eligibility for special education."
  3. List Suspected Disabilities: Detail specific concerns (dyslexia, ADHD, autism, speech delays) and interventions that have failed
  4. Create a Paper Trail: Email the request (time-stamped) or send via certified mail with return receipt

The 45 School-Day Timeline

Once Houston ISD receives your written request, they must follow strict timelines:

Within 15 school days: The campus must provide you with a written response and formal consent form to sign.

Within 45 school days of consent: The district must complete the Full Individual Evaluation (FIE) and provide you with a written report.

Within 30 calendar days of report: Houston ISD must hold the initial ARD meeting to determine eligibility.

Note: School days exclude weekends, holidays, and summer breaks. Track these dates carefully.

What the Evaluation Must Cover

The FIE is a comprehensive assessment by qualified professionals. It cannot be a single test. The evaluation must address all areas of suspected disability:

  • Academic Achievement: Testing for reading, writing, and math deficits
  • Cognitive Abilities: Intelligence, working memory, problem-solving skills
  • Related Services Needs: Speech/language, occupational therapy, social-emotional development

If Houston ISD Refuses to Evaluate

The district can refuse your request, but they must provide formal "Prior Written Notice" explaining:

  • Why they are refusing
  • What data they relied on
  • Your right to challenge the decision

If the district refuses or you disagree with their evaluation results, you have two options:

  1. Request an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at the district's expense
  2. File for a due process hearing to force the evaluation

Common Roadblocks to Avoid

RTI Cannot Delay Evaluation: Schools often suggest placing children in "Response to Intervention" (RTI) instead of testing for special education. RTI cannot be used to delay or deny a special education evaluation. You can request an FIE immediately.

You Don't Need Permission: You do not need the teacher's or principal's permission to request an evaluation. It is your federal right as a parent.

Document Everything: Keep copies of all correspondence, consent forms, and evaluation reports. You'll need this paper trail if disputes arise.

What Happens After the Evaluation

Once the FIE is complete, Houston ISD will schedule an ARD meeting to:

  • Review the evaluation results
  • Determine if your child qualifies for special education
  • Create an IEP if eligible

You have the right to invite anyone to this meeting, including advocates, attorneys, or private specialists.

Dyslexia Services in Houston ISD

What are my child's dyslexia rights in Texas?
Under the Texas Dyslexia Handbook, all districts must screen students for dyslexia in grades K-2 and provide evidence-based intervention programs. If identified, your child is entitled to structured literacy instruction and—if eligible—a 504 Plan or IEP with accommodations.

The Texas Dyslexia Handbook (2024 Update)

Houston ISD must follow the Texas Dyslexia Handbook, which provides the legal framework for identifying and serving students with dyslexia.

The 2024 update (following HB 3928) clarifies that a dyslexia evaluation is now officially a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) under special education law. This gives parents stronger legal protections.

Key point: If your child is struggling to read, you do not have to "wait and see." You can request a formal dyslexia evaluation in writing at any time.

Mandatory Screening Requirements

Texas law requires Houston ISD to screen all students in kindergarten and first grade for dyslexia. The screening evaluates:

  • Phonological awareness
  • Rapid naming
  • Letter-sound knowledge

If your child shows risk during screening, the campus must notify you and begin monitoring. However, you don't need to wait for a screener to flag your child—you can request a full evaluation immediately.

Structured Literacy Programs

If your child is identified with dyslexia, Houston ISD must provide a "Structured Literacy" program—an explicit, systematic approach to reading instruction.

Structured Literacy programs emphasize:

  • Phonemic awareness
  • Phonics
  • Fluency
  • Vocabulary
  • Reading comprehension

Instruction must be sequential and cumulative, building on previously learned skills. The specific program used will be determined in collaboration with your ARD or 504 committee.

504 Plan vs. IEP for Dyslexia

A major point of confusion: Should your dyslexic child receive a 504 Plan or a full IEP?

The 2024 handbook clarifies that dyslexia is a Specific Learning Disability (SLD), which qualifies for special education services under IDEA.

Section 504 Plan:

  • Appropriate for students who need accommodations (extra time, audio support) but can access the general curriculum with minimal support
  • Provides accommodations but not specialized instruction
  • Fewer legal protections than an IEP

IEP (Special Education):

  • Required for students whose dyslexia significantly impacts learning and who need intensive, individualized, specialized instruction
  • Provides both accommodations AND specialized teaching
  • Stronger legal protections and goals

If your child's reading is significantly below grade level, push for an IEP, not just a 504.

How to Request a Dyslexia Evaluation

Do not rely on verbal conversations. To start the legal clock, submit a written request:

  1. Write the Request: Draft a letter requesting a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) for suspected dyslexia and Specific Learning Disabilities
  2. Submit to the Principal: Email directly to your campus principal and copy the Special Education Director
  3. Watch the Clock: Once received, the district has 15 school days to provide written response and consent form
  4. Prepare for Testing: After you sign consent, the district has 45 school days to complete the evaluation

What Happens If the School Refuses?

If Houston ISD refuses to evaluate, they must provide "Prior Written Notice" explaining why. You can challenge this refusal by:

  • Filing a formal grievance with the district
  • Requesting mediation through TEA
  • Filing for a due process hearing

The school cannot force your child to go through months of "RTI" before agreeing to evaluate for dyslexia.

Accommodations for Dyslexia

Whether your child has a 504 or IEP, common accommodations include:

  • Extended time on tests and assignments
  • Audio books and text-to-speech software
  • Preferential seating near instruction
  • Reduced copying from board
  • Use of graphic organizers
  • Tests read aloud

Discipline Rights & Grievances in Houston ISD

⚠️ Urgent: If your child has been suspended for more than 10 cumulative days in Houston ISD, this is legally a Change of Placement. The school must hold an ARD/MDR meeting immediately.
Is a 10-day suspension a Change of Placement?
Yes. Under IDEA, suspending a special education student for more than 10 cumulative school days in a year triggers a Change of Placement and requires a Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) within 10 business days.

The 10-Day Suspension Rule

If your child has an IEP or 504 plan, standard discipline rules do not fully apply. Houston ISD can suspend your child for up to 10 days total per academic year.

After the 10th day of suspension (even if they are separate 2-day or 3-day incidents), federal law triggers massive protections:

  • The district MUST provide educational services to maintain academic progress
  • A Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) is required before any further disciplinary action
  • The school cannot send your child home with nothing

Important: Informal removals count too. If the principal keeps calling you to "pick your child up early" for behavior, those count as suspension days.

What Is an MDR (Manifestation Determination Review)?

An MDR is a mandatory meeting that occurs before Houston ISD can expel your child or send them to DAEP (Disciplinary Alternative Education Program) for more than 10 days.

The ARD committee must answer two questions:

  1. Was the conduct caused by, or directly related to, the child's disability?
  2. Was the conduct the direct result of Houston ISD's failure to implement the IEP?

If the answer to EITHER question is yes, the school cannot discipline your child the same way they would a non-disabled student. They must:

  • Conduct a Functional Behavioral Assessment (FBA)
  • Return your child to their original placement
  • Add behavioral supports to the IEP

Filing a Level 1 Grievance in Houston ISD

If you believe a campus administrator violated your child's rights or ignored their IEP, file a formal grievance. Angry emails don't count.

Steps to file a grievance:

  1. Find the Policy: Houston ISD board policy (FNG Local) governs complaints. You typically have only 15 days from the incident to file.
  2. Get the Form: Request the official Level 1 Grievance form from the principal or download from the district website.
  3. Stick to Facts: State the facts, the specific policy or IEP provision violated, and the exact remedy you seek.
  4. Submit Officially: Submit to the campus principal via email (keep a time-stamped copy).

TEA State Complaints

If the grievance process fails and Houston ISD continues to deny your child a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE), escalate to the state.

You can file a formal State Complaint with the Texas Education Agency (TEA), which triggers a state investigator to review the district's actions.

When to file a TEA complaint:

  • District refuses to evaluate your child
  • IEP services are not being implemented
  • District violated procedural safeguards (no 5-day notice, refused 10-day recess, etc.)
  • Child is being illegally suspended

TEA must investigate and issue findings. If violations are found, Houston ISD must take corrective action.

Due Process Hearings

For severe placement disagreements or if TEA complaints don't resolve the issue, you can file for a Due Process Hearing—a formal legal trial before an administrative judge.

This is the most powerful legal tool parents have, but it requires significant preparation. Most parents hire a special education attorney for due process.

Common Violations to Watch For

  • IEP not implemented: Services listed on IEP aren't actually being provided
  • No progress on goals: Child makes no measurable progress year after year
  • Informal suspensions: Repeatedly sent home early without formal suspension paperwork
  • Refused evaluation: District refuses to test despite written parent request
  • No prior written notice: District makes changes without giving you formal notice

Protecting Your Child's Placement

While disputes are being resolved, your child has the right to "stay-put"—they remain in their current educational placement unless you agree otherwise or a hearing officer orders a change.

The school cannot unilaterally move your child to a more restrictive setting while you're fighting for services.

ARD Meeting Guide: Houston ISD

What is an ARD meeting?
An ARD (Admission, Review, and Dismissal) meeting is Texas's IEP meeting where the school and parents decide your child's special education services. You must receive written notice at least 5 calendar days before the meeting and can request a 10-day recess at any time.

What Is an ARD Meeting?

An ARD meeting is required by federal law (IDEA) and Texas Education Code. It determines if your child qualifies for special education, creates their IEP, and reviews progress.

If your child attends Houston ISD, you are a legal member of the ARD committee with equal standing to principals and diagnosticians. The district cannot make decisions about your child's education without you.

Your 5-Day Notice Right

You have the right to receive written notice at least 5 calendar days before any ARD meeting. This gives you time to prepare, gather documents, or arrange for an advocate.

The notice must state the meeting's purpose (Annual Review, Change of Placement, etc.), location, and who will attend from the district.

Note: You can waive the 5-day notice in writing for emergencies, but the school cannot pressure you to do so.

The 10-Day Recess Rule

If you feel overwhelmed or need time to review data, Texas law gives you a powerful tool: the 10-day recess.

At any point during the meeting, state: "I am requesting a 10-day recess to review this information." Houston ISD must grant it, pause the meeting, and reconvene within 10 school days.

Use this when:

  • The IEP draft looks wrong
  • You need to consult an expert
  • You're being rushed to sign
  • New information surprises you

  2. Written List of Concerns: You'll forget your talking points in the moment
  3. Previous IEPs & Progress Reports: Prove whether goals were actually met

  • Accommodations: Extended time, preferential seating, graphic organizers, etc.

When You Disagree With the Team

You can disagree with the ARD committee's recommendations. If you disagree with initial placement, you can refuse consent—services cannot begin without your signature.

If you disagree with a revised IEP:

  • Do NOT sign "Agree"
  • Check "Disagree"
  • Request your 10-day recess
  • Ensure your concerns are written in the official minutes

You also have the right to request an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at district expense if you believe their testing is flawed.

Document Everything: Keep copies of all correspondence, consent forms, and evaluation reports. You'll need this paper trail if disputes arise.

What Happens After the Evaluation

Once the FIE is complete, Houston ISD will schedule an ARD meeting to:

  • Review the evaluation results
  • Determine if your child qualifies for special education
  • Create an IEP if eligible

What are my child's dyslexia rights in Texas?
Under the Texas Dyslexia Handbook, all districts must screen students for dyslexia in grades K-2 and provide evidence-based intervention programs. If identified, your child is entitled to structured literacy instruction and—if eligible—a 504 Plan or IEP with accommodations.

The Texas Dyslexia Handbook (2024 Update)

Houston ISD must follow the Texas Dyslexia Handbook, which provides the legal framework for identifying and serving students with dyslexia.

The 2024 update (following HB 3928) clarifies that a dyslexia evaluation is now officially a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) under special education law. This gives parents stronger legal protections.

Key point: If your child is struggling to read, you do not have to "wait and see." You can request a formal dyslexia evaluation in writing at any time.

Mandatory Screening Requirements

Texas law requires Houston ISD to screen all students in kindergarten and first grade for dyslexia. The screening evaluates:

  • Phonological awareness
  • Rapid naming
  • Letter-sound knowledge

If your child shows risk during screening, the campus must notify you and begin monitoring. However, you don't need to wait for a screener to flag your child—you can request a full evaluation immediately.

Structured Literacy Programs

If your child is identified with dyslexia, Houston ISD must provide a "Structured Literacy" program—an explicit, systematic approach to reading instruction.

Structured Literacy programs emphasize:

  • Phonemic awareness
  • Phonics
  • Fluency
  • Vocabulary
  • Reading comprehension

Instruction must be sequential and cumulative, building on previously learned skills. The specific program used will be determined in collaboration with your ARD or 504 committee.

  • Appropriate for students who need accommodations (extra time, audio support) but can access the general curriculum with minimal support

  • Required for students whose dyslexia significantly impacts learning and who need intensive, individualized, specialized instruction
  • Provides both accommodations AND specialized teaching
  • Stronger legal protections and goals

How to Request a Dyslexia Evaluation

  4. Prepare for Testing: After you sign consent, the district has 45 school days to complete the evaluation

  • Requesting mediation through TEA

Discipline Rights & Grievances in Houston ISD

⚠️ Urgent: If your child has been suspended for more than 10 cumulative days in Houston ISD, this is legally a Change of Placement. The school must hold an ARD/MDR meeting immediately.
Is a 10-day suspension a Change of Placement?
Yes. Under IDEA, suspending a special education student for more than 10 cumulative school days in a year triggers a Change of Placement and requires a Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) within 10 business days.

The 10-Day Suspension Rule

If your child has an IEP or 504 plan, standard discipline rules do not fully apply. Houston ISD can suspend your child for up to 10 days total per academic year.

After the 10th day of suspension (even if they are separate 2-day or 3-day incidents), federal law triggers massive protections:

  • The district MUST provide educational services to maintain academic progress
  • A Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) is required before any further disciplinary action
  • The school cannot send your child home with nothing

Important: Informal removals count too. If the principal keeps calling you to "pick your child up early" for behavior, those count as suspension days.

What Is an MDR (Manifestation Determination Review)?

An MDR is a mandatory meeting that occurs before Houston ISD can expel your child or send them to DAEP (Disciplinary Alternative Education Program) for more than 10 days.

The ARD committee must answer two questions:

  1. Was the conduct caused by, or directly related to, the child's disability?
  2. Was the conduct the direct result of Houston ISD's failure to implement the IEP?

If the answer to EITHER question is yes, the school cannot discipline your child the same way they would a non-disabled student. They must:

  • Conduct a Functional Behavioral Assessment (FBA)
  • Return your child to their original placement
  • Add behavioral supports to the IEP

Filing a Level 1 Grievance in Houston ISD

If you believe a campus administrator violated your child's rights or ignored their IEP, file a formal grievance. Angry emails don't count.

Steps to file a grievance:

  1. Find the Policy: Houston ISD board policy (FNG Local) governs complaints. You typically have only 15 days from the incident to file.
  2. Get the Form: Request the official Level 1 Grievance form from the principal or download from the district website.
  3. Stick to Facts: State the facts, the specific policy or IEP provision violated, and the exact remedy you seek.
  4. Submit Officially: Submit to the campus principal via email (keep a time-stamped copy).

TEA State Complaints

If the grievance process fails and Houston ISD continues to deny your child a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE), escalate to the state.

You can file a formal State Complaint with the Texas Education Agency (TEA), which triggers a state investigator to review the district's actions.

When to file a TEA complaint:

  • District refuses to evaluate your child
  • IEP services are not being implemented
  • District violated procedural safeguards (no 5-day notice, refused 10-day recess, etc.)
  • Child is being illegally suspended

TEA must investigate and issue findings. If violations are found, Houston ISD must take corrective action.

Due Process Hearings

For severe placement disagreements or if TEA complaints don't resolve the issue, you can file for a Due Process Hearing—a formal legal trial before an administrative judge.

This is the most powerful legal tool parents have, but it requires significant preparation. Most parents hire a special education attorney for due process.

Common Violations to Watch For

  • IEP not implemented: Services listed on IEP aren't actually being provided
  • No progress on goals: Child makes no measurable progress year after year
  • Informal suspensions: Repeatedly sent home early without formal suspension paperwork
  • Refused evaluation: District refuses to test despite written parent request
  • No prior written notice: District makes changes without giving you formal notice

Protecting Your Child's Placement

While disputes are being resolved, your child has the right to "stay-put"—they remain in their current educational placement unless you agree otherwise or a hearing officer orders a change.

The school cannot unilaterally move your child to a more restrictive setting while you're fighting for services.

ARD Meeting Guide: Houston ISD

What is an ARD meeting?
An ARD (Admission, Review, and Dismissal) meeting is Texas's IEP meeting where the school and parents decide your child's special education services. You must receive written notice at least 5 calendar days before the meeting and can request a 10-day recess at any time.

What Is an ARD Meeting?

An ARD meeting is required by federal law (IDEA) and Texas Education Code. It determines if your child qualifies for special education, creates their IEP, and reviews progress.

If your child attends Houston ISD, you are a legal member of the ARD committee with equal standing to principals and diagnosticians. The district cannot make decisions about your child's education without you.

Your 5-Day Notice Right

You have the right to receive written notice at least 5 calendar days before any ARD meeting. This gives you time to prepare, gather documents, or arrange for an advocate.

The notice must state the meeting's purpose (Annual Review, Change of Placement, etc.), location, and who will attend from the district.

Note: You can waive the 5-day notice in writing for emergencies, but the school cannot pressure you to do so.

The 10-Day Recess Rule

If you feel overwhelmed or need time to review data, Texas law gives you a powerful tool: the 10-day recess.

At any point during the meeting, state: "I am requesting a 10-day recess to review this information." Houston ISD must grant it, pause the meeting, and reconvene within 10 school days.

Use this when:

  • The IEP draft looks wrong
  • You need to consult an expert
  • You're being rushed to sign
  • New information surprises you

What to Bring to Your ARD

Come prepared to shift the balance of power:

  1. Outside Medical Records: Letters from private therapists, psychologists, or pediatricians
  2. Written List of Concerns: You'll forget your talking points in the moment
  3. Previous IEPs & Progress Reports: Prove whether goals were actually met
  4. A Support Person: You can bring an advocate, friend, or attorney

Understanding the IEP Document

The IEP is a legally binding contract. Pay attention to these sections:

  • Present Levels (PLAAFP): Your child's current performance. Push back if vague.
  • Annual Goals: Must be measurable. "Will improve reading" is not legal. "Will read 90 words per minute by May" is.
  • Services: Exact minutes per week for each service (Speech, Resource Room, etc.)
  • Accommodations: Extended time, preferential seating, graphic organizers, etc.

When You Disagree With the Team

You can disagree with the ARD committee's recommendations. If you disagree with initial placement, you can refuse consent—services cannot begin without your signature.

If you disagree with a revised IEP:

  • Do NOT sign "Agree"
  • Check "Disagree"
  • Request your 10-day recess
  • Ensure your concerns are written in the official minutes

You also have the right to request an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at district expense if you believe their testing is flawed.

After the ARD Meeting

Houston ISD must provide you with a copy of the finalized IEP within a reasonable time. Services should begin as soon as possible after you sign consent for initial services.

If the district fails to implement the IEP as written, document every violation and file a formal grievance or TEA complaint.

Requesting a Special Education Evaluation

If your child is struggling academically or you suspect a disability, you have the absolute legal right to request a special education evaluation from Houston ISD.

Under IDEA's "Child Find" provisions, Houston ISD must identify, locate, and evaluate all children with disabilities. Your formal written request triggers this legal obligation.

Important: Verbal requests to a teacher do not start the legal clock. You must put your evaluation request in writing.

How to Submit Your Written Request

  1. Address It Correctly: Send the letter to both the campus principal and the Special Education Director
  2. Use the "Magic Words": State clearly: "I am requesting a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) under IDEA to determine eligibility for special education."
  3. List Suspected Disabilities: Detail specific concerns (dyslexia, ADHD, autism, speech delays) and interventions that have failed
  4. Create a Paper Trail: Email the request (time-stamped) or send via certified mail with return receipt

The 45 School-Day Timeline

Once Houston ISD receives your written request, they must follow strict timelines:

Within 15 school days: The campus must provide you with a written response and formal consent form to sign.

Within 45 school days of consent: The district must complete the Full Individual Evaluation (FIE) and provide you with a written report.

Within 30 calendar days of report: Houston ISD must hold the initial ARD meeting to determine eligibility.

Note: School days exclude weekends, holidays, and summer breaks. Track these dates carefully.

  • Academic Achievement: Testing for reading, writing, and math deficits
  • Cognitive Abilities: Intelligence, working memory, problem-solving skills
  • Related Services Needs: Speech/language, occupational therapy, social-emotional development

  • Why they are refusing
  • What data they relied on

  • Review the evaluation results
  • Determine if your child qualifies for special education
  • Create an IEP if eligible

  • Filing for a due process hearing

The school cannot force your child to go through months of "RTI" before agreeing to evaluate for dyslexia.

  • Extended time on tests and assignments
  • Audio books and text-to-speech software
  • Preferential seating near instruction
  • Reduced copying from board
  • Use of graphic organizers
  • Tests read aloud

Discipline Rights & Grievances in Houston ISD

⚠️ Urgent: If your child has been suspended for more than 10 cumulative days in Houston ISD, this is legally a Change of Placement. The school must hold an ARD/MDR meeting immediately.
Is a 10-day suspension a Change of Placement?
Yes. Under IDEA, suspending a special education student for more than 10 cumulative school days in a year triggers a Change of Placement and requires a Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) within 10 business days.

The 10-Day Suspension Rule

If your child has an IEP or 504 plan, standard discipline rules do not fully apply. Houston ISD can suspend your child for up to 10 days total per academic year.

After the 10th day of suspension (even if they are separate 2-day or 3-day incidents), federal law triggers massive protections:

  • The district MUST provide educational services to maintain academic progress
  • A Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) is required before any further disciplinary action
  • The school cannot send your child home with nothing

Important: Informal removals count too. If the principal keeps calling you to "pick your child up early" for behavior, those count as suspension days.

What Is an MDR (Manifestation Determination Review)?

An MDR is a mandatory meeting that occurs before Houston ISD can expel your child or send them to DAEP (Disciplinary Alternative Education Program) for more than 10 days.

The ARD committee must answer two questions:

  1. Was the conduct caused by, or directly related to, the child's disability?
  2. Was the conduct the direct result of Houston ISD's failure to implement the IEP?

If the answer to EITHER question is yes, the school cannot discipline your child the same way they would a non-disabled student. They must:

  • Conduct a Functional Behavioral Assessment (FBA)
  • Return your child to their original placement
  • Add behavioral supports to the IEP

Filing a Level 1 Grievance in Houston ISD

If you believe a campus administrator violated your child's rights or ignored their IEP, file a formal grievance. Angry emails don't count.

Steps to file a grievance:

  1. Find the Policy: Houston ISD board policy (FNG Local) governs complaints. You typically have only 15 days from the incident to file.
  2. Get the Form: Request the official Level 1 Grievance form from the principal or download from the district website.
  3. Stick to Facts: State the facts, the specific policy or IEP provision violated, and the exact remedy you seek.
  4. Submit Officially: Submit to the campus principal via email (keep a time-stamped copy).

TEA State Complaints

If the grievance process fails and Houston ISD continues to deny your child a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE), escalate to the state.

You can file a formal State Complaint with the Texas Education Agency (TEA), which triggers a state investigator to review the district's actions.

When to file a TEA complaint:

  • District refuses to evaluate your child
  • IEP services are not being implemented
  • District violated procedural safeguards (no 5-day notice, refused 10-day recess, etc.)
  • Child is being illegally suspended

TEA must investigate and issue findings. If violations are found, Houston ISD must take corrective action.

Due Process Hearings

For severe placement disagreements or if TEA complaints don't resolve the issue, you can file for a Due Process Hearing—a formal legal trial before an administrative judge.

This is the most powerful legal tool parents have, but it requires significant preparation. Most parents hire a special education attorney for due process.

Common Violations to Watch For

  • IEP not implemented: Services listed on IEP aren't actually being provided
  • No progress on goals: Child makes no measurable progress year after year
  • Informal suspensions: Repeatedly sent home early without formal suspension paperwork
  • Refused evaluation: District refuses to test despite written parent request
  • No prior written notice: District makes changes without giving you formal notice

Protecting Your Child's Placement

While disputes are being resolved, your child has the right to "stay-put"—they remain in their current educational placement unless you agree otherwise or a hearing officer orders a change.

The school cannot unilaterally move your child to a more restrictive setting while you're fighting for services.

ARD Meeting Guide: Houston ISD

What is an ARD meeting?
An ARD (Admission, Review, and Dismissal) meeting is Texas's IEP meeting where the school and parents decide your child's special education services. You must receive written notice at least 5 calendar days before the meeting and can request a 10-day recess at any time.

What Is an ARD Meeting?

An ARD meeting is required by federal law (IDEA) and Texas Education Code. It determines if your child qualifies for special education, creates their IEP, and reviews progress.

If your child attends Houston ISD, you are a legal member of the ARD committee with equal standing to principals and diagnosticians. The district cannot make decisions about your child's education without you.

Your 5-Day Notice Right

You have the right to receive written notice at least 5 calendar days before any ARD meeting. This gives you time to prepare, gather documents, or arrange for an advocate.

The notice must state the meeting's purpose (Annual Review, Change of Placement, etc.), location, and who will attend from the district.

Note: You can waive the 5-day notice in writing for emergencies, but the school cannot pressure you to do so.

The 10-Day Recess Rule

If you feel overwhelmed or need time to review data, Texas law gives you a powerful tool: the 10-day recess.

At any point during the meeting, state: "I am requesting a 10-day recess to review this information." Houston ISD must grant it, pause the meeting, and reconvene within 10 school days.

Use this when:

  • The IEP draft looks wrong
  • You need to consult an expert
  • You're being rushed to sign
  • New information surprises you

What to Bring to Your ARD

Come prepared to shift the balance of power:

  1. Outside Medical Records: Letters from private therapists, psychologists, or pediatricians
  2. Written List of Concerns: You'll forget your talking points in the moment
  3. Previous IEPs & Progress Reports: Prove whether goals were actually met
  4. A Support Person: You can bring an advocate, friend, or attorney

Understanding the IEP Document

The IEP is a legally binding contract. Pay attention to these sections:

  • Present Levels (PLAAFP): Your child's current performance. Push back if vague.
  • Annual Goals: Must be measurable. "Will improve reading" is not legal. "Will read 90 words per minute by May" is.
  • Services: Exact minutes per week for each service (Speech, Resource Room, etc.)
  • Accommodations: Extended time, preferential seating, graphic organizers, etc.

When You Disagree With the Team

You can disagree with the ARD committee's recommendations. If you disagree with initial placement, you can refuse consent—services cannot begin without your signature.

If you disagree with a revised IEP:

  • Do NOT sign "Agree"
  • Check "Disagree"
  • Request your 10-day recess
  • Ensure your concerns are written in the official minutes

You also have the right to request an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at district expense if you believe their testing is flawed.

After the ARD Meeting

Houston ISD must provide you with a copy of the finalized IEP within a reasonable time. Services should begin as soon as possible after you sign consent for initial services.

If the district fails to implement the IEP as written, document every violation and file a formal grievance or TEA complaint.

How to Request an Evaluation in Houston ISD

How long does Texas have to complete an evaluation?
Once Houston ISD receives your written evaluation request, they have exactly 45 school days to complete the Full Individual Evaluation (FIE) and hold an ARD meeting. Verbal requests don't start the clock—always submit in writing.

Requesting a Special Education Evaluation

If your child is struggling academically or you suspect a disability, you have the absolute legal right to request a special education evaluation from Houston ISD.

Under IDEA's "Child Find" provisions, Houston ISD must identify, locate, and evaluate all children with disabilities. Your formal written request triggers this legal obligation.

Important: Verbal requests to a teacher do not start the legal clock. You must put your evaluation request in writing.

How to Submit Your Written Request

Follow these steps to ensure your request is legally valid:

  1. Address It Correctly: Send the letter to both the campus principal and the Special Education Director
  2. Use the "Magic Words": State clearly: "I am requesting a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) under IDEA to determine eligibility for special education."
  3. List Suspected Disabilities: Detail specific concerns (dyslexia, ADHD, autism, speech delays) and interventions that have failed
  4. Create a Paper Trail: Email the request (time-stamped) or send via certified mail with return receipt

The 45 School-Day Timeline

Once Houston ISD receives your written request, they must follow strict timelines:

Within 15 school days: The campus must provide you with a written response and formal consent form to sign.

Within 45 school days of consent: The district must complete the Full Individual Evaluation (FIE) and provide you with a written report.

Within 30 calendar days of report: Houston ISD must hold the initial ARD meeting to determine eligibility.

Note: School days exclude weekends, holidays, and summer breaks. Track these dates carefully.

What the Evaluation Must Cover

The FIE is a comprehensive assessment by qualified professionals. It cannot be a single test. The evaluation must address all areas of suspected disability:

  • Academic Achievement: Testing for reading, writing, and math deficits
  • Cognitive Abilities: Intelligence, working memory, problem-solving skills
  • Related Services Needs: Speech/language, occupational therapy, social-emotional development

If Houston ISD Refuses to Evaluate

The district can refuse your request, but they must provide formal "Prior Written Notice" explaining:

  • Why they are refusing
  • What data they relied on
  • Your right to challenge the decision

If the district refuses or you disagree with their evaluation results, you have two options:

  1. Request an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at the district's expense
  2. File for a due process hearing to force the evaluation

Common Roadblocks to Avoid

RTI Cannot Delay Evaluation: Schools often suggest placing children in "Response to Intervention" (RTI) instead of testing for special education. RTI cannot be used to delay or deny a special education evaluation. You can request an FIE immediately.

You Don't Need Permission: You do not need the teacher's or principal's permission to request an evaluation. It is your federal right as a parent.

Document Everything: Keep copies of all correspondence, consent forms, and evaluation reports. You'll need this paper trail if disputes arise.

What Happens After the Evaluation

Once the FIE is complete, Houston ISD will schedule an ARD meeting to:

  • Review the evaluation results
  • Determine if your child qualifies for special education
  • Create an IEP if eligible

You have the right to invite anyone to this meeting, including advocates, attorneys, or private specialists.

Dyslexia Services in Houston ISD

What are my child's dyslexia rights in Texas?
Under the Texas Dyslexia Handbook, all districts must screen students for dyslexia in grades K-2 and provide evidence-based intervention programs. If identified, your child is entitled to structured literacy instruction and—if eligible—a 504 Plan or IEP with accommodations.

The Texas Dyslexia Handbook (2024 Update)

Houston ISD must follow the Texas Dyslexia Handbook, which provides the legal framework for identifying and serving students with dyslexia.

The 2024 update (following HB 3928) clarifies that a dyslexia evaluation is now officially a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) under special education law. This gives parents stronger legal protections.

Key point: If your child is struggling to read, you do not have to "wait and see." You can request a formal dyslexia evaluation in writing at any time.

Mandatory Screening Requirements

Texas law requires Houston ISD to screen all students in kindergarten and first grade for dyslexia. The screening evaluates:

  • Phonological awareness
  • Rapid naming
  • Letter-sound knowledge

If your child shows risk during screening, the campus must notify you and begin monitoring. However, you don't need to wait for a screener to flag your child—you can request a full evaluation immediately.

Structured Literacy Programs

If your child is identified with dyslexia, Houston ISD must provide a "Structured Literacy" program—an explicit, systematic approach to reading instruction.

Structured Literacy programs emphasize:

  • Phonemic awareness
  • Phonics
  • Fluency
  • Vocabulary
  • Reading comprehension

Instruction must be sequential and cumulative, building on previously learned skills. The specific program used will be determined in collaboration with your ARD or 504 committee.

504 Plan vs. IEP for Dyslexia

A major point of confusion: Should your dyslexic child receive a 504 Plan or a full IEP?

The 2024 handbook clarifies that dyslexia is a Specific Learning Disability (SLD), which qualifies for special education services under IDEA.

Section 504 Plan:

  • Appropriate for students who need accommodations (extra time, audio support) but can access the general curriculum with minimal support
  • Provides accommodations but not specialized instruction
  • Fewer legal protections than an IEP

IEP (Special Education):

  • Required for students whose dyslexia significantly impacts learning and who need intensive, individualized, specialized instruction
  • Provides both accommodations AND specialized teaching
  • Stronger legal protections and goals

If your child's reading is significantly below grade level, push for an IEP, not just a 504.

How to Request a Dyslexia Evaluation

Do not rely on verbal conversations. To start the legal clock, submit a written request:

  1. Write the Request: Draft a letter requesting a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) for suspected dyslexia and Specific Learning Disabilities
  2. Submit to the Principal: Email directly to your campus principal and copy the Special Education Director
  3. Watch the Clock: Once received, the district has 15 school days to provide written response and consent form
  4. Prepare for Testing: After you sign consent, the district has 45 school days to complete the evaluation

What Happens If the School Refuses?

If Houston ISD refuses to evaluate, they must provide "Prior Written Notice" explaining why. You can challenge this refusal by:

  • Filing a formal grievance with the district
  • Requesting mediation through TEA
  • Filing for a due process hearing

The school cannot force your child to go through months of "RTI" before agreeing to evaluate for dyslexia.

Accommodations for Dyslexia

Whether your child has a 504 or IEP, common accommodations include:

  • Extended time on tests and assignments
  • Audio books and text-to-speech software
  • Preferential seating near instruction
  • Reduced copying from board
  • Use of graphic organizers
  • Tests read aloud

ARD Meeting Guide: Houston ISD

What is an ARD meeting?
An ARD (Admission, Review, and Dismissal) meeting is Texas's IEP meeting where the school and parents decide your child's special education services. You must receive written notice at least 5 calendar days before the meeting and can request a 10-day recess at any time.

What Is an ARD Meeting?

An ARD meeting is required by federal law (IDEA) and Texas Education Code. It determines if your child qualifies for special education, creates their IEP, and reviews progress.

If your child attends Houston ISD, you are a legal member of the ARD committee with equal standing to principals and diagnosticians. The district cannot make decisions about your child's education without you.

Your 5-Day Notice Right

You have the right to receive written notice at least 5 calendar days before any ARD meeting. This gives you time to prepare, gather documents, or arrange for an advocate.

The notice must state the meeting's purpose (Annual Review, Change of Placement, etc.), location, and who will attend from the district.

Note: You can waive the 5-day notice in writing for emergencies, but the school cannot pressure you to do so.

The 10-Day Recess Rule

If you feel overwhelmed or need time to review data, Texas law gives you a powerful tool: the 10-day recess.

At any point during the meeting, state: "I am requesting a 10-day recess to review this information." Houston ISD must grant it, pause the meeting, and reconvene within 10 school days.

Use this when:

  • The IEP draft looks wrong
  • You need to consult an expert
  • You're being rushed to sign
  • New information surprises you

What to Bring to Your ARD

Come prepared to shift the balance of power:

  1. Outside Medical Records: Letters from private therapists, psychologists, or pediatricians
  2. Written List of Concerns: You'll forget your talking points in the moment
  3. Previous IEPs & Progress Reports: Prove whether goals were actually met
  4. A Support Person: You can bring an advocate, friend, or attorney

Understanding the IEP Document

The IEP is a legally binding contract. Pay attention to these sections:

  • Present Levels (PLAAFP): Your child's current performance. Push back if vague.
  • Annual Goals: Must be measurable. "Will improve reading" is not legal. "Will read 90 words per minute by May" is.
  • Services: Exact minutes per week for each service (Speech, Resource Room, etc.)
  • Accommodations: Extended time, preferential seating, graphic organizers, etc.

When You Disagree With the Team

You can disagree with the ARD committee's recommendations. If you disagree with initial placement, you can refuse consent—services cannot begin without your signature.

If you disagree with a revised IEP:

  • Do NOT sign "Agree"
  • Check "Disagree"
  • Request your 10-day recess
  • Ensure your concerns are written in the official minutes

You also have the right to request an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at district expense if you believe their testing is flawed.

After the ARD Meeting

Houston ISD must provide you with a copy of the finalized IEP within a reasonable time. Services should begin as soon as possible after you sign consent for initial services.

If the district fails to implement the IEP as written, document every violation and file a formal grievance or TEA complaint.

How to Request an Evaluation in Houston ISD

How long does Texas have to complete an evaluation?
Once Houston ISD receives your written evaluation request, they have exactly 45 school days to complete the Full Individual Evaluation (FIE) and hold an ARD meeting. Verbal requests don't start the clock—always submit in writing.

Requesting a Special Education Evaluation

If your child is struggling academically or you suspect a disability, you have the absolute legal right to request a special education evaluation from Houston ISD.

Under IDEA's "Child Find" provisions, Houston ISD must identify, locate, and evaluate all children with disabilities. Your formal written request triggers this legal obligation.

Important: Verbal requests to a teacher do not start the legal clock. You must put your evaluation request in writing.

How to Submit Your Written Request

Follow these steps to ensure your request is legally valid:

  1. Address It Correctly: Send the letter to both the campus principal and the Special Education Director
  2. Use the "Magic Words": State clearly: "I am requesting a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) under IDEA to determine eligibility for special education."
  3. List Suspected Disabilities: Detail specific concerns (dyslexia, ADHD, autism, speech delays) and interventions that have failed
  4. Create a Paper Trail: Email the request (time-stamped) or send via certified mail with return receipt

The 45 School-Day Timeline

Once Houston ISD receives your written request, they must follow strict timelines:

Within 15 school days: The campus must provide you with a written response and formal consent form to sign.

Within 45 school days of consent: The district must complete the Full Individual Evaluation (FIE) and provide you with a written report.

Within 30 calendar days of report: Houston ISD must hold the initial ARD meeting to determine eligibility.

Note: School days exclude weekends, holidays, and summer breaks. Track these dates carefully.

What the Evaluation Must Cover

The FIE is a comprehensive assessment by qualified professionals. It cannot be a single test. The evaluation must address all areas of suspected disability:

  • Academic Achievement: Testing for reading, writing, and math deficits
  • Cognitive Abilities: Intelligence, working memory, problem-solving skills
  • Related Services Needs: Speech/language, occupational therapy, social-emotional development

If Houston ISD Refuses to Evaluate

The district can refuse your request, but they must provide formal "Prior Written Notice" explaining:

  • Why they are refusing
  • What data they relied on
  • Your right to challenge the decision

If the district refuses or you disagree with their evaluation results, you have two options:

  1. Request an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at the district's expense
  2. File for a due process hearing to force the evaluation

Common Roadblocks to Avoid

RTI Cannot Delay Evaluation: Schools often suggest placing children in "Response to Intervention" (RTI) instead of testing for special education. RTI cannot be used to delay or deny a special education evaluation. You can request an FIE immediately.

You Don't Need Permission: You do not need the teacher's or principal's permission to request an evaluation. It is your federal right as a parent.

Document Everything: Keep copies of all correspondence, consent forms, and evaluation reports. You'll need this paper trail if disputes arise.

What Happens After the Evaluation

Once the FIE is complete, Houston ISD will schedule an ARD meeting to:

  • Review the evaluation results
  • Determine if your child qualifies for special education
  • Create an IEP if eligible

You have the right to invite anyone to this meeting, including advocates, attorneys, or private specialists.

Dyslexia Services in Houston ISD

What are my child's dyslexia rights in Texas?
Under the Texas Dyslexia Handbook, all districts must screen students for dyslexia in grades K-2 and provide evidence-based intervention programs. If identified, your child is entitled to structured literacy instruction and—if eligible—a 504 Plan or IEP with accommodations.

The Texas Dyslexia Handbook (2024 Update)

Houston ISD must follow the Texas Dyslexia Handbook, which provides the legal framework for identifying and serving students with dyslexia.

The 2024 update (following HB 3928) clarifies that a dyslexia evaluation is now officially a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) under special education law. This gives parents stronger legal protections.

Key point: If your child is struggling to read, you do not have to "wait and see." You can request a formal dyslexia evaluation in writing at any time.

Mandatory Screening Requirements

Texas law requires Houston ISD to screen all students in kindergarten and first grade for dyslexia. The screening evaluates:

  • Phonological awareness
  • Rapid naming
  • Letter-sound knowledge

If your child shows risk during screening, the campus must notify you and begin monitoring. However, you don't need to wait for a screener to flag your child—you can request a full evaluation immediately.

Structured Literacy Programs

If your child is identified with dyslexia, Houston ISD must provide a "Structured Literacy" program—an explicit, systematic approach to reading instruction.

Structured Literacy programs emphasize:

  • Phonemic awareness
  • Phonics
  • Fluency
  • Vocabulary
  • Reading comprehension

Instruction must be sequential and cumulative, building on previously learned skills. The specific program used will be determined in collaboration with your ARD or 504 committee.

504 Plan vs. IEP for Dyslexia

A major point of confusion: Should your dyslexic child receive a 504 Plan or a full IEP?

The 2024 handbook clarifies that dyslexia is a Specific Learning Disability (SLD), which qualifies for special education services under IDEA.

Section 504 Plan:

  • Appropriate for students who need accommodations (extra time, audio support) but can access the general curriculum with minimal support
  • Provides accommodations but not specialized instruction
  • Fewer legal protections than an IEP

IEP (Special Education):

  • Required for students whose dyslexia significantly impacts learning and who need intensive, individualized, specialized instruction
  • Provides both accommodations AND specialized teaching
  • Stronger legal protections and goals

If your child's reading is significantly below grade level, push for an IEP, not just a 504.

How to Request a Dyslexia Evaluation

Do not rely on verbal conversations. To start the legal clock, submit a written request:

  1. Write the Request: Draft a letter requesting a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) for suspected dyslexia and Specific Learning Disabilities
  2. Submit to the Principal: Email directly to your campus principal and copy the Special Education Director
  3. Watch the Clock: Once received, the district has 15 school days to provide written response and consent form
  4. Prepare for Testing: After you sign consent, the district has 45 school days to complete the evaluation

What Happens If the School Refuses?

If Houston ISD refuses to evaluate, they must provide "Prior Written Notice" explaining why. You can challenge this refusal by:

  • Filing a formal grievance with the district
  • Requesting mediation through TEA
  • Filing for a due process hearing

The school cannot force your child to go through months of "RTI" before agreeing to evaluate for dyslexia.

Accommodations for Dyslexia

Whether your child has a 504 or IEP, common accommodations include:

  • Extended time on tests and assignments
  • Audio books and text-to-speech software
  • Preferential seating near instruction
  • Reduced copying from board
  • Use of graphic organizers
  • Tests read aloud

Discipline Rights & Grievances in Houston ISD

⚠️ Urgent: If your child has been suspended for more than 10 cumulative days in Houston ISD, this is legally a Change of Placement. The school must hold an ARD/MDR meeting immediately.
Is a 10-day suspension a Change of Placement?
Yes. Under IDEA, suspending a special education student for more than 10 cumulative school days in a year triggers a Change of Placement and requires a Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) within 10 business days.

The 10-Day Suspension Rule

If your child has an IEP or 504 plan, standard discipline rules do not fully apply. Houston ISD can suspend your child for up to 10 days total per academic year.

After the 10th day of suspension (even if they are separate 2-day or 3-day incidents), federal law triggers massive protections:

  • The district MUST provide educational services to maintain academic progress
  • A Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) is required before any further disciplinary action
  • The school cannot send your child home with nothing

Important: Informal removals count too. If the principal keeps calling you to "pick your child up early" for behavior, those count as suspension days.

What Is an MDR (Manifestation Determination Review)?

An MDR is a mandatory meeting that occurs before Houston ISD can expel your child or send them to DAEP (Disciplinary Alternative Education Program) for more than 10 days.

The ARD committee must answer two questions:

  1. Was the conduct caused by, or directly related to, the child's disability?
  2. Was the conduct the direct result of Houston ISD's failure to implement the IEP?

If the answer to EITHER question is yes, the school cannot discipline your child the same way they would a non-disabled student. They must:

  • Conduct a Functional Behavioral Assessment (FBA)
  • Return your child to their original placement
  • Add behavioral supports to the IEP

Filing a Level 1 Grievance in Houston ISD

If you believe a campus administrator violated your child's rights or ignored their IEP, file a formal grievance. Angry emails don't count.

Steps to file a grievance:

  1. Find the Policy: Houston ISD board policy (FNG Local) governs complaints. You typically have only 15 days from the incident to file.
  2. Get the Form: Request the official Level 1 Grievance form from the principal or download from the district website.
  3. Stick to Facts: State the facts, the specific policy or IEP provision violated, and the exact remedy you seek.
  4. Submit Officially: Submit to the campus principal via email (keep a time-stamped copy).

TEA State Complaints

If the grievance process fails and Houston ISD continues to deny your child a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE), escalate to the state.

You can file a formal State Complaint with the Texas Education Agency (TEA), which triggers a state investigator to review the district's actions.

When to file a TEA complaint:

  • District refuses to evaluate your child
  • IEP services are not being implemented
  • District violated procedural safeguards (no 5-day notice, refused 10-day recess, etc.)
  • Child is being illegally suspended

TEA must investigate and issue findings. If violations are found, Houston ISD must take corrective action.

Due Process Hearings

For severe placement disagreements or if TEA complaints don't resolve the issue, you can file for a Due Process Hearing—a formal legal trial before an administrative judge.

This is the most powerful legal tool parents have, but it requires significant preparation. Most parents hire a special education attorney for due process.

Common Violations to Watch For

  • IEP not implemented: Services listed on IEP aren't actually being provided
  • No progress on goals: Child makes no measurable progress year after year
  • Informal suspensions: Repeatedly sent home early without formal suspension paperwork
  • Refused evaluation: District refuses to test despite written parent request
  • No prior written notice: District makes changes without giving you formal notice

Protecting Your Child's Placement

While disputes are being resolved, your child has the right to "stay-put"—they remain in their current educational placement unless you agree otherwise or a hearing officer orders a change.

The school cannot unilaterally move your child to a more restrictive setting while you're fighting for services.

ARD Meeting Guide: Houston ISD

What is an ARD meeting?
An ARD (Admission, Review, and Dismissal) meeting is Texas's IEP meeting where the school and parents decide your child's special education services. You must receive written notice at least 5 calendar days before the meeting and can request a 10-day recess at any time.

What Is an ARD Meeting?

An ARD meeting is required by federal law (IDEA) and Texas Education Code. It determines if your child qualifies for special education, creates their IEP, and reviews progress.

If your child attends Houston ISD, you are a legal member of the ARD committee with equal standing to principals and diagnosticians. The district cannot make decisions about your child's education without you.

Your 5-Day Notice Right

You have the right to receive written notice at least 5 calendar days before any ARD meeting. This gives you time to prepare, gather documents, or arrange for an advocate.

The notice must state the meeting's purpose (Annual Review, Change of Placement, etc.), location, and who will attend from the district.

Note: You can waive the 5-day notice in writing for emergencies, but the school cannot pressure you to do so.

The 10-Day Recess Rule

If you feel overwhelmed or need time to review data, Texas law gives you a powerful tool: the 10-day recess.

At any point during the meeting, state: "I am requesting a 10-day recess to review this information." Houston ISD must grant it, pause the meeting, and reconvene within 10 school days.

Use this when:

  • The IEP draft looks wrong
  • You need to consult an expert
  • You're being rushed to sign
  • New information surprises you

What to Bring to Your ARD

Come prepared to shift the balance of power:

  1. Outside Medical Records: Letters from private therapists, psychologists, or pediatricians
  2. Written List of Concerns: You'll forget your talking points in the moment
  3. Previous IEPs & Progress Reports: Prove whether goals were actually met
  4. A Support Person: You can bring an advocate, friend, or attorney

Understanding the IEP Document

The IEP is a legally binding contract. Pay attention to these sections:

  • Present Levels (PLAAFP): Your child's current performance. Push back if vague.
  • Annual Goals: Must be measurable. "Will improve reading" is not legal. "Will read 90 words per minute by May" is.
  • Services: Exact minutes per week for each service (Speech, Resource Room, etc.)
  • Accommodations: Extended time, preferential seating, graphic organizers, etc.

When You Disagree With the Team

You can disagree with the ARD committee's recommendations. If you disagree with initial placement, you can refuse consent—services cannot begin without your signature.

If you disagree with a revised IEP:

  • Do NOT sign "Agree"
  • Check "Disagree"
  • Request your 10-day recess
  • Ensure your concerns are written in the official minutes

You also have the right to request an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at district expense if you believe their testing is flawed.

After the ARD Meeting

Houston ISD must provide you with a copy of the finalized IEP within a reasonable time. Services should begin as soon as possible after you sign consent for initial services.

If the district fails to implement the IEP as written, document every violation and file a formal grievance or TEA complaint.

How to Request an Evaluation in Houston ISD

How long does Texas have to complete an evaluation?
Once Houston ISD receives your written evaluation request, they have exactly 45 school days to complete the Full Individual Evaluation (FIE) and hold an ARD meeting. Verbal requests don't start the clock—always submit in writing.

Requesting a Special Education Evaluation

If your child is struggling academically or you suspect a disability, you have the absolute legal right to request a special education evaluation from Houston ISD.

Under IDEA's "Child Find" provisions, Houston ISD must identify, locate, and evaluate all children with disabilities. Your formal written request triggers this legal obligation.

Important: Verbal requests to a teacher do not start the legal clock. You must put your evaluation request in writing.

How to Submit Your Written Request

Follow these steps to ensure your request is legally valid:

  1. Address It Correctly: Send the letter to both the campus principal and the Special Education Director
  2. Use the "Magic Words": State clearly: "I am requesting a Full Individual and Initial Evaluation (FIIE) under IDEA to determine eligibility for special education."
  3. List Suspected Disabilities: Detail specific concerns (dyslexia, ADHD, autism, speech delays) and interventions that have failed
  4. Create a Paper Trail: Email the request (time-stamped) or send via certified mail with return receipt

The 45 School-Day Timeline

Once Houston ISD receives your written request, they must follow strict timelines:

Within 15 school days: The campus must provide you with a written response and formal consent form to sign.

Within 45 school days of consent: The district must complete the Full Individual Evaluation (FIE) and provide you with a written report.

Within 30 calendar days of report: Houston ISD must hold the initial ARD meeting to determine eligibility.

Note: School days exclude weekends, holidays, and summer breaks. Track these dates carefully.

What the Evaluation Must Cover

The FIE is a comprehensive assessment by qualified professionals. It cannot be a single test. The evaluation must address all areas of suspected disability:

  • Academic Achievement: Testing for reading, writing, and math deficits
  • Cognitive Abilities: Intelligence, working memory, problem-solving skills
  • Related Services Needs: Speech/language, occupational therapy, social-emotional development

If Houston ISD Refuses to Evaluate

The district can refuse your request, but they must provide formal "Prior Written Notice" explaining:

  • Why they are refusing
  • What data they relied on
  • Your right to challenge the decision

If the district refuses or you disagree with their evaluation results, you have two options:

  1. Request an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at the district's expense
  2. File for a due process hearing to force the evaluation

Common Roadblocks to Avoid

RTI Cannot Delay Evaluation: Schools often suggest placing children in "Response to Intervention" (RTI) instead of testing for special education. RTI cannot be used to delay or deny a special education evaluation. You can request an FIE immediately.

You Don't Need Permission: You do not need the teacher's or principal's permission to request an evaluation. It is your federal right as a parent.

Document Everything: Keep copies of all correspondence, consent forms, and evaluation reports. You'll need this paper trail if disputes arise.

What Happens After the Evaluation

Once the FIE is complete, Houston ISD will schedule an ARD meeting to:

  • Review the evaluation results
  • Determine if your child qualifies for special education
  • Create an IEP if eligible

You have the right to invite anyone to this meeting, including advocates, attorneys, or private specialists.
